nameMeanings.name: Yate
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: (i) locative name, occupational name for someone who lived by a gate (Middle English yat, Old English geat), in some cases because they acted as the gatekeeper or porter to a hall, a monastery, or some other important building. Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames mentions a witness to a set of deeds who is variously named as John ad portam, John le Porter, and John atteyate, 1322–6 in Yorks Deeds (Wadworth, WR Yorks). Compare Yeatman , Gate locative name for someone who lived at or came from Yate (Gloucs).

variants.name: Yorks Deeds
meanings: Norman, English: nickname from Anglo-Norman French dobler, dubler ‘a kind of dish’ (Old French doblier, doublier), probably used to denote a maker of plates or dishes; compare Adam le Dublerwrite, 1249 in Assize Rolls (Wilts), Hugh le dublerwrith, 14th cent. Yorks Deeds I.

variants.name: Yeatman
meanings: English (southern): occupational name for a gate-keeper, from Middle English yate + man. Compare Yates .

variants.name: Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames
meanings: English: locative name, probably from Gilderdale (Forest) in Alston (Cumb), as suggested by   Redmonds, Dictionary of Yorks Surnames  , rather than from Gildersdale in Warter (ER Yorks), as suggested by   Reaney and Wilson  .

variants.name: Yates
meanings: English: from Middle English yates ‘gates’, plural of yate, Old English geat ‘gate’, hence a topographic or occupational name for someone who lived by the gates of a town or castle and who probably acted as the gatekeeper or porter. Compare Gates . Alternatively, a variant of the singular form Yate, with post-medieval excrescent -s.

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

en.lifeSummary: Mrs. Robert Yate was born in 1312, in Charney Bassett, Berkshire, England, United Kingdom. She had at least 1 son with Robert Yates. She died in her hometown.
